ach_question: Which hypothesis best explains the agency's theory of responsibility for the adulteration of the firm's drug products?

hypotheses.label: Systemic quality-system / process failure
hypotheses.claim: The firm's own quality system is broadly inadequate — across incoming-component testing, production/process control, and quality-unit oversight — so responsibility for the adulteration rests on systemic CGMP failures at the firm.

hypotheses.label: Vendor/supplier fault
hypotheses.claim: The real source of risk is upstream: contaminated or substandard raw materials (methanol in ethanol, DEG/EG in glycerin) come from suppliers, so responsibility lies with the component suppliers rather than the firm.

hypotheses.label: Specific bad decision (the unvalidated water system)
hypotheses.claim: The problem is one discrete decision — using an unvalidated '(b)(4) water' system — rather than a firm-wide quality failure.

hypotheses.label: Regulatory-status problem (unapproved/misbranded/unlisted)
hypotheses.claim: The firm's core problem is regulatory status: marketing an unapproved new drug, misbranding, and failing to keep its drug listings current.

evidence_rows.label: Identity-testing failure on incoming components
evidence_rows.text_anchor: You failed to adequately perform identity testing on each shipment of each lot of incoming components (e.g., ethanol, glycerin)
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Pillar 1 of systemic quality (h1). Also the locus of the vendor tension (h2), since the components come from suppliers.

evidence_rows.label: Firm is responsible despite supplier COAs
evidence_rows.text_anchor: You are responsible for sampling, testing, and examining drug components before use in production to ensure acceptable quality parameters are met
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: CAUSAL BRIDGE. Keeps responsibility with the firm rather than the supplier: reliance on supplier COAs without establishing their reliability is the firm's failure. Most directly defeats h2.

evidence_rows.label: Process-control / water-system failure
evidence_rows.text_anchor: You lacked water system validation and failed to conduct total organic carbon (TOC) testing
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Pillar 2 of systemic quality (h1). Independent of the component-testing pillar. Also the row a reader could over-read as a single specific decision (h3).

evidence_rows.label: Quality-unit oversight failure
evidence_rows.text_anchor: Your quality unit (QU) failed to perform adequate oversight for the manufacture and testing of your OTC topical drug products
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Pillar 3 of systemic quality (h1): the QU itself — the function responsible for catching all of the above — failed, including batch-record and lab-record review. Strongest support that the failure is systemic, not a one-off.

evidence_rows.label: Upstream contamination hazard (DEG/EG, methanol)
evidence_rows.text_anchor: incoming components at high risk of diethylene glycol (DEG) or ethylene glycol (EG) contamination
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Supports h2 on its face — the hazardous impurities originate in raw materials. Double-edged: the order uses the hazard to explain why the firm must test, reinforcing h1.

evidence_rows.label: Secure-supply-chain CAPA request
evidence_rows.text_anchor: ensuring that all incoming raw material lots are from fully qualified manufacturers and free from unsafe impurities
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Double-edged. Acknowledges the supply-chain dimension (h2) but frames qualifying suppliers as the firm's own CAPA duty (h1).

evidence_rows.label: Unapproved new drug / misbranding / listing inactivation
evidence_rows.source_coords: [UNAPPROVED NEW DRUG AND MISBRANDED DRUG VIOLATIONS]; [DRUG LISTING VIOLATIONS]
evidence_rows.text_anchor: an unapproved new drug marketed in violation of sections 505(a) and 301(d)
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: OFF-AXIS. True and serious, but these are regulatory-status violations (approval, labeling, listing), not the CGMP-adulteration responsibility theory the ACH question asks about. Supports h4, which is off-axis.

evidence_rows.label: Executive management responsible for systemic flaws
evidence_rows.text_anchor: executive management remains responsible for resolving all deficiencies and systemic flaws
evidence_rows.expected_relevance: Supports h1: the FDA frames the matter as firm-wide systemic flaws (a six-system audit is recommended), not a single decision (h3) or a supplier problem (h2).

expected_read.rationale: The order locates responsibility in a systemic quality-system failure: three independent CGMP pillars are cited — incoming-component identity testing (e1), production/process control of the water system (e3), and quality-unit oversight including batch- and lab-record review (e4) — and the FDA frames the matter as firm-wide systemic flaws requiring a six-system audit (e8). The vendor reading (h2) is tempting because the DEG/EG and methanol hazards originate upstream (e5), but the order expressly keeps responsibility with the firm: it must sample and test incoming components and establish the reliability of supplier COAs (e2). The single-decision reading (h3) captures only one pillar (e3). The unapproved-drug, misbranding, and listing violations (e7) are off-axis: real, but a regulatory-status theory, not the adulteration/CGMP responsibility theory.
expected_read.flip_note: The winner survives any single-row removal because it rests on three independent pillars (e1, e3, e4). But it weakens meaningfully on the supplier axis if e2 (the firm-is-responsible-despite-COAs bridge) is removed: the identity-testing findings (e1) plus the upstream-hazard row (e5) then read more naturally as vendor fault (h2). Removing e2 alone does not flip the read — the process-control (e3) and quality-unit (e4) pillars still anchor h1 — but removing both e2 and e4 would create serious uncertainty between systemic-quality and vendor-fault. The off-axis h4 never wins on the adulteration question regardless of which rows are present.
